location_name: Utrecht, Dutch Republic (signed); implemented along West African coast and Spanish America
main_actor: British South Sea Company, under grant from the Spanish Crown (Treaty of Utrecht, article X of the British-Spanish Asiento contract)
description: As part of the Treaty of Utrecht that ended the War of Spanish Succession, Spain transferred the Asiento — the monopoly license to supply enslaved Africans to Spanish America — from French to British hands, granting the South Sea Company the right to ship 4,800 enslaved Africans per year for thirty years. The contract institutionalised British dominance of the trans-Atlantic slave trade for the eighteenth century; the Company in turn subcontracted to the Royal African Company and private traders operating on the Gold Coast, Bight of Benin and Loango coast. Over the century of the Asiento and successor arrangements, at least 3 million enslaved Africans were shipped to Spanish America alone.
casualties_source: Slave Voyages Database (slavevoyages.org), British ships to Spanish America 1713-1810
ethical_notes: Classified SLAVE_TRADE per ETHICS-007. The treaty itself is sometimes framed as a diplomatic achievement in European textbooks; here it is catalogued by its actual economic substance. Casualty count reflects aggregate shipped under the instrument and its extensions; mortality during the Middle Passage is reflected in the Slave Voyages dataset at roughly 12-15% per voyage.

entity_links.notes: Great Britain after 1707 Act of Union
grantor of the monopoly

sources.citation: Palmer, C.A. (1981). Human Cargoes: The British Slave Trade to Spanish America, 1700-1739. University of Illinois Press.
Eltis, D. & Richardson, D. (2010). Atlas of the Transatlantic Slave Trade. Yale University Press.
Treaty of Utrecht, Asiento Contract, 26 March 1713 (full text in Clive Parry, Consolidated Treaty Series, vol. 27).

location_name: Cape of Good Hope (Table Bay), modern South Africa
main_actor: Verenigde Oostindische Compagnie (Dutch East India Company) expedition under Jan van Riebeeck
description: On 6 April 1652 three VOC ships under Jan van Riebeeck landed at Table Bay to establish a victualling station for Dutch ships on the Asia route. The post developed rapidly into a settler colony which dispossessed Khoekhoe pastoralists through a series of cattle wars (1659-1660, 1673-1677), introduced slaves from Madagascar, India and Indonesia, and produced the Trekboer frontier culture ancestral to the Afrikaner nation. The 1652 landing is conventionally taken as the beginning of European settler colonialism in Southern Africa.
ethical_notes: The foundation is sometimes celebrated as a 'founding' of South Africa in older (pre-1994) Afrikaans-nationalist historiography; ETHICS-001 requires the Khoekhoe dispossession narrative to be present on equal footing. The indigenous name Camissa (Place of Sweet Waters) survives in parallel; the VOC outpost renamed it Kaap de Goede Hoop.

entity_links.notes: via Verenigde Oostindische Compagnie
long descent culminating in the 1961 Republic

sources.citation: Elphick, R. & Giliomee, H. (1989). The Shaping of South African Society, 1652-1840. Wesleyan University Press.
Van Riebeeck, J. (1952). Journal of Jan van Riebeeck (3 vols.). Cape Town: A.A. Balkema (primary source, transcribed and edited).
Mbeki, T. (1998). 'The African Renaissance, South Africa and the World' (address at UN University). For counter-narrative framing.

location_name: Kumasi, modern Ghana
main_actor: Osei Tutu I (Asantehene) and priest Okomfo Anokye, unifying Akan city-states
description: Following the defeat of the Denkyira overlords at the Battle of Feyiase (c. 1701), Osei Tutu consolidated a confederation of Akan oman (city-states) under the Golden Stool (Sika Dwa Kofi), which Okomfo Anokye is said to have caused to descend from the sky to embody the Asante soul. The Asante state developed a bureaucratic military empire that dominated the Gold Coast interior, traded gold and enslaved captives to European factors at Cape Coast and Elmina, and would resist British encroachment through four Anglo-Asante Wars (1824-1900).
ethical_notes: The Asante polity is catalogued as a sovereign state, not merely a 'tribal kingdom' (a nineteenth-century colonial euphemism used to deny diplomatic parity). Asante participated in the Atlantic slave trade as supplier of captives from interior wars; this aspect is part of the historical record and is not elided.

entity_links.notes: defeated suzerain

sources.citation: Wilks, I. (1975). Asante in the Nineteenth Century: The Structure and Evolution of a Political Order. Cambridge University Press.
McCaskie, T.C. (1995). State and Society in Pre-Colonial Asante. Cambridge University Press.

location_name: Southern Africa (Natal, Highveld, Mozambique, Zimbabwe)
main_actor: Shaka kaSenzangakhona of the Zulu kingdom; Mzilikazi of the Ndebele; Sobhuza I of the Swazi; Soshangane of the Gaza; Moshoeshoe I of the Basotho
description: A cascade of warfare and forced migration across southern Africa in the early nineteenth century, triggered by the rapid centralisation of the Zulu kingdom under Shaka (reigned 1816-1828) and his military innovations (iKlwa stabbing spear, age-regiment system). Secondary shockwaves drove the Ndebele north of the Limpopo, the Gaza into Mozambique, and generated new defensive polities such as Moshoeshoe's Basotho kingdom and Mswati's Swazi. Estimates of mortality range from 1 to 2 million across the period.
ethical_notes: The 'Mfecane' framing was weaponised by 20th-century apartheid historiography to argue that the Highveld was empty for Voortrekker seizure; revisionist scholarship (Julian Cobbing) pointed out that the Cape frontier slave-raid economy may have driven much of the destabilisation. The event is nonetheless historically real; the casualty range reflects deep uncertainty.

entity_links.notes: Northward-displaced peoples reached Lozi domains

sources.citation: Etherington, N. (2001). The Great Treks: The Transformation of Southern Africa, 1815-1854. Longman.
Cobbing, J. (1988). 'The Mfecane as Alibi: Thoughts on Dithakong and Mbolompo'. Journal of African History 29(3): 487-519.
Hamilton, C. (ed.) (1995). The Mfecane Aftermath: Reconstructive Debates in Southern African History. Witwatersrand University Press.

location_name: British Kaffraria and the Transkei, modern Eastern Cape, South Africa
main_actor: Xhosa prophetess Nongqawuse and King Sarhili (Sarili) of the Gcaleka Xhosa
description: In April 1856 the young Xhosa prophetess Nongqawuse reported visions from the spirits of the Xhosa ancestors demanding the slaughter of all cattle and destruction of all grain stores; in return the dead would rise, the British would be swept into the sea, and an age of plenty would begin. Between April 1856 and early 1857 the Xhosa killed approximately 400,000 cattle and left fields unplanted. When the resurrection failed to occur, famine killed 40,000 to 75,000 Xhosa (roughly a third to half of the population) and forced tens of thousands into wage labour in the Cape Colony, a social catastrophe that the Cape governor Sir George Grey exploited to break Xhosa political independence.
casualties_source: Peires, J.B. (1989). The Dead Will Arise
silence_reason: The cattle-killing is routinely described in English-language sources as Xhosa millenarian 'superstition', which elides two silenced contexts: (1) the preceding lungsickness epidemic of 1853-54 that had already decimated Xhosa herds and gave the prophecy material plausibility, and (2) Governor Grey's deliberate exploitation of the resulting famine to dismantle Xhosa political independence, which Peires documents from British colonial records. The Xhosa side of the event is preserved largely in oral tradition collected only in the twentieth century.
ethical_notes: Classified OTHER rather than FAMINE because the proximate cause was religious-political, not climatic; the famine was a downstream consequence. ETHICS-008: Xhosa oral sources preserved the event; colonial sources framed it as cultural self-destruction. Peires (1989) reconstructs both.

entity_links.notes: Cape Colony governor exploited the famine

sources.citation: Peires, J.B. (1989). The Dead Will Arise: Nongqawuse and the Great Xhosa Cattle-Killing Movement of 1856-7. Ravan Press.
Bradford, H. (2008). 'Akukho Ntaka Inokubhabha Ngephiko Elinye: The Abolition of the Dowry and the Cattle-Killing'. Kronos 34.

location_name: Adwa, Tigray, modern Ethiopia
main_actor: Emperor Menelik II and Empress Taytu Betul of Ethiopia, commanding a coalition army of Shewan, Tigrayan, Amhara, Oromo and Gurage levies
description: On 1 March 1896 a 100,000-strong Ethiopian army decisively defeated an Italian colonial force of approximately 17,000 under General Oreste Baratieri, inflicting ~7,000 Italian killed and capturing ~3,000. The battle forced Italy to abrogate the Treaty of Wuchale and recognise Ethiopian sovereignty in the Treaty of Addis Ababa (October 1896), making Ethiopia the only African polity to preserve independence during the Scramble. Italian askari (Eritrean colonial troops) captured by the Ethiopians were mutilated as punishment for treason.
casualties_source: Berkeley, G.F.-H. (1902). The Campaign of Adowa
ethical_notes: Adwa is iconic in Pan-African and Rastafarian historiography as the reversal of the Scramble narrative. Italian historiography until the 1930s framed it as a national humiliation that justified the 1935 invasion (catalogued separately). The askari mutilations are part of the record and are not hidden here.

entity_links.notes: Ethiopian Empire under Menelik II
defeated

sources.citation: Jonas, R. (2011). The Battle of Adwa: African Victory in the Age of Empire. Harvard University Press.
Berkeley, G.F.-H. (1902). The Campaign of Adowa and the Rise of Menelik. Archibald Constable.
Pankhurst, R. (1998). The Ethiopians: A History. Blackwell.

name_original: Ruanga rwa Kongo libre / Atrocités de l'État indépendant du Congo
location_name: Congo Basin (Congo Free State), modern Democratic Republic of the Congo
main_actor: King Leopold II of the Belgians as personal sovereign of the Congo Free State; Force Publique; concessionary rubber companies (ABIR, Anversoise)
description: Under Leopold II's personal rule (1885-1908) the Congo Free State extracted ivory and wild rubber through a regime of quotas enforced by hostage-taking, village burnings, severing of hands for failed quotas, and mass executions by the Force Publique and its African auxiliaries. Population estimates for 1885 are contested (10 to 20 million), but the 1920 colonial census of the successor Belgian Congo recorded roughly 10 million; demographers attribute a population loss of 8 to 10 million to violence, starvation, disease and collapsing birth rates. International exposure through the Casement Report (1904) and the Congo Reform Association forced the transfer to Belgian state sovereignty in 1908.
casualties_source: Hochschild, A. (1998). King Leopold's Ghost (citing Jan Vansina, demographic estimates)
silence_reason: For twenty years (1885-1904) the Free State actively suppressed independent reporting; Leopold II maintained a Brussels press bureau that denied all allegations. Full archives of the Free State were burnt in 1908 before transfer to the Belgian state. The scale of mortality was denied in Belgian public discourse until the 1999 publication of Hochschild, and only formally acknowledged by the Belgian state in the 2020 royal regret expressed by King Philippe.
ethical_notes: This entry is distinct from the 1885 FOUNDATION of the Free State (already catalogued as 'État Indépendant du Congo' in the DB); this record catalogues the atrocities themselves as a COLONIAL_VIOLENCE event spanning 1885-1908. The term 'genocide' is used by some scholars (e.g. Adam Hochschild accepts it qualified) but is contested because targeting was economic-extractive rather than ethnic; ETHICS-007 COLONIAL_VIOLENCE is retained here.

entity_links.notes: Among many affected peoples: Kongo, Mongo, Tetela, Luba, Zande, Yaka, Pende

sources.citation: Hochschild, A. (1998). King Leopold's Ghost: A Story of Greed, Terror, and Heroism in Colonial Africa. Houghton Mifflin.
Casement, R. (1904). Report of the British Consul on the Administration of the Congo Free State. UK Parliamentary Papers (Cd. 1933).
Vansina, J. (2010). Being Colonized: The Kuba Experience in Rural Congo, 1880-1960. University of Wisconsin Press.

location_name: German East Africa (modern southern Tanzania)
main_actor: Coalition of Matumbi, Ngindo, Pogoro, Bena, Ngoni and other peoples under the prophet Kinjikitile Ngwale; suppressed by the German Schutztruppe under Governor Gustav Adolf von Götzen
description: Triggered by forced cotton cultivation on German settler plantations and head-tax burdens, a multi-ethnic rebellion swept southern German East Africa from July 1905. The prophet Kinjikitile distributed maji (water) believed to turn German bullets to water; though Kinjikitile himself was hanged in August 1905 the movement spread. The German response was a two-year scorched-earth campaign that burnt villages and destroyed food stores, causing famine that killed 200,000 to 300,000. The 1907 suppression depopulated southern Tanzania for a generation and became a foundational memory of Tanzanian anticolonial nationalism under Nyerere.
casualties_source: Iliffe, J. (1979). A Modern History of Tanganyika
ethical_notes: Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE per ETHICS-007 because the bulk of mortality came from the German famine-inducing campaign, not battlefield deaths. Some scholarship (e.g. Felicitas Becker) treats it as a genocidal famine; this batch retains COLONIAL_VIOLENCE but notes the debate.

entity_links.notes: suppressing power

sources.citation: Iliffe, J. (1979). A Modern History of Tanganyika. Cambridge University Press.
Becker, F. & Beez, J. (eds.) (2005). Der Maji-Maji-Krieg in Deutsch-Ostafrika 1905-1907. Christoph Links Verlag.

location_name: Ethiopia (invasion from Italian Eritrea and Italian Somaliland)
main_actor: Kingdom of Italy under Benito Mussolini; Regio Esercito under Marshals Emilio De Bono, Pietro Badoglio and Rodolfo Graziani
description: On 3 October 1935 Italian forces invaded Ethiopia from Eritrea and Italian Somaliland in violation of League of Nations obligations. Despite resistance from Emperor Haile Selassie, Italy used mustard gas sprayed from aircraft, bombing of Red Cross hospitals, and mass civilian reprisals (notably the February 1937 Addis Ababa massacre after an assassination attempt on Graziani, in which ~20,000 Ethiopians were killed by Italian carabinieri and fascist squadristi). Formal annexation to Italian East Africa was proclaimed in May 1936; effective control never extended beyond main roads and towns, and the occupation ended with British liberation in 1941.
casualties_source: Del Boca, A. (1976-1984). Gli italiani in Africa Orientale (4 vols.)
silence_reason: The use of chemical weapons was denied by the Italian government for decades; Mussolini's 1935-36 telegrams ordering mustard-gas deployment were only published by Del Boca in 1996 and fully accepted as historical fact after documentary evidence from the Italian Historical Diplomatic Archive. The Addis Ababa massacre of February 1937 (Yekatit 12) was also systematically minimised in post-war Italian public memory.
ethical_notes: Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE rather than CONQUEST because the chemical-weapon use and civilian reprisals (explicit violations of the 1925 Geneva Protocol which Italy had ratified) define the event's character. Some Ethiopian historiography argues for GENOCIDE; the lower classification here reflects ETHICS-007 caution given contested scholarship.

sources.citation: Del Boca, A. (1976-1984). Gli italiani in Africa Orientale (4 vols.). Laterza.
Del Boca, A. (1996). I gas di Mussolini: Il fascismo e la guerra d'Etiopia. Editori Riuniti.
Mockler, A. (1984). Haile Selassie's War. Oxford University Press.

location_name: Sharpeville township, Transvaal, South Africa
main_actor: South African Police under the apartheid government of Hendrik Verwoerd
description: On 21 March 1960 approximately 7,000 unarmed protesters gathered outside the Sharpeville police station, part of a Pan Africanist Congress (PAC) campaign against the pass laws. South African Police opened fire on the crowd without warning, killing 69 people (many shot in the back while fleeing) and wounding 180. The response was a state of emergency, the banning of the ANC and PAC on 8 April 1960, and the commitment of both movements to armed struggle (MK and Poqo). Sharpeville is the hinge-point from which the international anti-apartheid movement dates, and 21 March is observed as UN International Day for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination.
casualties_source: Report of the Commission of Enquiry into the Occurrences at Sharpeville and Langa (1960)
ethical_notes: The apartheid-era official narrative framed the shooting as legitimate crowd-control response to an 'attack' on the police station; the Commission of Enquiry (chaired by Justice Wessels) found no evidence of such an attack. The Truth and Reconciliation Commission revisited the event in 1996.

entity_links.notes: Union of South Africa at the time (Republic from 1961)

sources.citation: Frankel, P. (2001). An Ordinary Atrocity: Sharpeville and Its Massacre. Yale University Press.
Report of the Commission of Enquiry into the Occurrences at Sharpeville and Langa (Wessels Commission), 1960.

location_name: Élisabethville, Katanga (modern Lubumbashi, Democratic Republic of the Congo)
main_actor: Katangese secessionist regime of Moïse Tshombe; Belgian military advisers and police officers; with foreknowledge of the US CIA and Belgian government
description: On 17 January 1961 Patrice Lumumba — the democratically elected first Prime Minister of the newly independent Republic of the Congo (30 June 1960) — was flown under Belgian guard from Léopoldville to Élisabethville in secessionist Katanga, beaten in transit, and executed by firing squad that evening under the supervision of Belgian Police Commissioner Frans Verscheure and Katangese gendarmes. The 2001 Belgian Parliamentary Commission established that the Belgian government bore 'moral responsibility' for the killing. The US CIA had independently planned to kill Lumumba (documented in the Church Committee report of 1975), though the Belgian-Katangese operation pre-empted it.
ethical_notes: English-language accounts long framed the killing as an internal Katangese affair; the Belgian and CIA roles were acknowledged by those states only in 2001 and 1975 respectively. Classified OTHER as the EventType enum lacks 'political assassination' as a label.

entity_links.notes: Belgian state — not a French entity; Belgium does not appear in the entity list

sources.citation: De Witte, L. (2001). The Assassination of Lumumba. Verso.

Belgian Chamber of Representatives (2001). Rapport fait au nom de la Commission d'enquête parlementaire chargée de déterminer les circonstances exactes de l'assassinat de Patrice Lumumba. Doc. 50 0312/007.
US Senate Select Committee to Study Governmental Operations (Church Committee), Interim Report: Alleged Assassination Plots Involving Foreign Leaders (1975).

location_name: French Algeria (including Aurès, Kabylia, Algiers, the Sahara)
main_actor: Front de Libération Nationale (FLN) and its Armée de Libération Nationale; French army, colonial police, harkis, and the pied-noir OAS
description: On 1 November 1954 the FLN launched coordinated attacks across Algeria (Toussaint Rouge), beginning an eight-year war against French rule. The conflict saw systematic French military use of torture (documented in the 1957 Battle of Algiers by Paratroopers under General Jacques Massu), forced population resettlement of ~2 million Algerians into internment camps, and massacres on both sides. The Évian Accords of 18 March 1962 ended the war; Algerian independence was proclaimed on 5 July 1962. Algerian sources cite ~1.5 million martyrs (a political figure); demographic estimates range 300,000 to 500,000 Algerian dead. ~1 million pieds-noirs and harkis fled to France; abandoned harkis were massacred en masse after independence.
casualties_source: Stora, B. (2004). Histoire de la guerre d'Algérie; Horne, A. (1977). A Savage War of Peace
silence_reason: French state acknowledgment was extraordinarily slow: the conflict was not officially termed a 'war' in France until 18 October 1999 (law 99-882). Use of torture was denied by successive French governments until President Macron's 2018 acknowledgment regarding the death of Maurice Audin. The post-independence harki massacres (estimates 55,000-150,000) remain under-researched in both French and Algerian historiography.
ethical_notes: Classified REVOLUTION following Algerian and postcolonial historiography; French state terminology (guerre d'Algérie) and British-US Cold War framing (counterinsurgency) are both downstream of the same event. Casualty range reflects the political uses of the 'million martyrs' figure vs. demographic reconstruction.

entity_links.notes: suppressing power; Algeria was juridically French departments

sources.citation: Stora, B. (2004). Histoire de la guerre d'Algérie (1954-1962). La Découverte.
Horne, A. (1977). A Savage War of Peace: Algeria 1954-1962. Macmillan.
Branche, R. (2001). La torture et l'armée pendant la guerre d'Algérie, 1954-1962. Gallimard.

location_name: Republic of Biafra (southeastern Nigeria: Igbo, Ibibio, Ijaw regions)
main_actor: Federal Military Government of Nigeria under General Yakubu Gowon; Republic of Biafra under Colonel Chukwuemeka Odumegwu Ojukwu
description: After the May 1966 and September 1966 pogroms in northern Nigeria that killed ~30,000 Igbo, Colonel Ojukwu declared the secessionist Republic of Biafra on 30 May 1967. The Federal Military Government of Nigeria imposed a naval and land blockade; relief flights organised by the Joint Church Aid (Caritas, World Council of Churches) were sporadically authorised or attacked. Mass starvation — kwashiorkor and marasmus — killed an estimated 1 to 3 million civilians, overwhelmingly Igbo children. Biafra surrendered on 15 January 1970 after the fall of Owerri.
casualties_source: Achebe, C. (2012). There Was a Country; Heerten & Moses (2014)
silence_reason: Post-war Nigerian state policy under the 'no victor, no vanquished' doctrine systematically suppressed Biafran memorialisation; public commemoration was prosecuted in Nigeria until the 1999 democratic transition, and remains contested. The blockade-starvation aspect was denied by the British government (which supplied arms to Federal Nigeria) and only fully acknowledged in the 2000s Biafra historiographical revival.
ethical_notes: Contested classification: some scholarship (e.g. Achebe, Ekwe-Ekwe) argues for GENOCIDE per the 1948 UN Genocide Convention given the targeted blockade of an ethnic region. This batch retains COLONIAL_VIOLENCE given active scholarly debate; the classification may be revisited as consensus shifts.

sources.citation: Achebe, C. (2012). There Was a Country: A Personal History of Biafra. Penguin.
Heerten, L. & Moses, A.D. (eds.) (2014). Postcolonial Conflict and the Question of Genocide: The Nigeria-Biafra War, 1967-1970. Routledge.
Stremlau, J. (1977). The International Politics of the Nigerian Civil War, 1967-1970. Princeton University Press.

location_name: Lisbon, Portugal; Luanda, Maputo, Bissau, São Tomé, Praia
main_actor: Movimento das Forças Armadas (MFA) in Portugal; MPLA and FNLA/UNITA in Angola; FRELIMO in Mozambique; PAIGC in Guinea-Bissau and Cape Verde
description: On 25 April 1974 junior officers of the MFA overthrew the Estado Novo regime of Marcelo Caetano in Lisbon, ending thirteen years of colonial wars in Angola (from 1961), Guinea-Bissau (from 1963), and Mozambique (from 1964). Between September 1974 and November 1975 Portugal granted independence to Guinea-Bissau (10 September 1974), Mozambique (25 June 1975), Cape Verde (5 July 1975), São Tomé and Príncipe (12 July 1975), and Angola (11 November 1975). Angolan decolonisation collapsed immediately into a Cold-War proxy civil war (MPLA vs. UNITA/FNLA, with Cuban, Soviet, South African and US intervention) that lasted until 2002.
ethical_notes: Catalogued as a chain event because the Portuguese colonial wars (1961-1974) and their 1974-75 resolution form a single historical unit; granular subevents (MPLA Luanda declaration, RENAMO/FRELIMO war) can be catalogued separately as follow-ons.

entity_links.notes: Portuguese authoritarian regime overthrown
post-1910 Republic; entity in DB is the monarchical predecessor

sources.citation: Newitt, M. (1981). Portugal in Africa: The Last Hundred Years. Longman.
MacQueen, N. (1997). The Decolonization of Portuguese Africa: Metropolitan Revolution and the Dissolution of Empire. Longman.

location_name: Soweto, Johannesburg, South Africa
main_actor: South African Police; organised students of the South African Students Movement (SASM) and Soweto Students Representative Council led by Tsietsi Mashinini
description: On 16 June 1976, between 3,000 and 10,000 Soweto students marched to protest the Bantu Education decree requiring half of secondary-school instruction to be in Afrikaans. South African Police opened fire on the unarmed marchers at Orlando West; the image of dying 12-year-old Hector Pieterson carried by Mbuyisa Makhubu (photograph by Sam Nzima) became iconic. Unrest spread to some 100 townships nationally over the following year, killing 575 to 700 (Cillie Commission figures) — overwhelmingly township youth shot by police. 16 June is now observed as Youth Day in South Africa.
ethical_notes: The official Cillie Commission figure is widely regarded as a floor; township activist estimates reach 1,000. Classified MASSACRE (of the 16 June protest itself) rather than REBELLION to match the unarmed civilian target profile; the wider township unrest is a follow-on.

sources.citation: Ndlovu, S.M. (1998). The Soweto Uprisings: Counter-Memories of June 1976. Ravan Press.
Cillie Commission (1980). Report of the Commission of Inquiry into the Riots at Soweto and Elsewhere. Government Printer, Pretoria.

location_name: Tigray and Wollo provinces, Ethiopia
main_actor: Derg regime of Mengistu Haile Mariam; compounded by drought and the Tigray civil war
description: A combination of two-year drought, counterinsurgency policy targeting rebel-held areas of Tigray, and the forced 'villagisation' and resettlement programme of the Derg regime produced a famine that killed between 400,000 and 1 million Ethiopians. The Derg's denial of international access to Tigrayan TPLF areas through 1984, and diversion of food aid to government-controlled towns, were documented at the time by Médecins Sans Frontières and later by Human Rights Watch. The famine reached Western public awareness through Michael Buerk's BBC reports of October 1984 and the Live Aid concerts of 1985.
casualties_source: Human Rights Watch (1991). Evil Days: 30 Years of War and Famine in Ethiopia
silence_reason: Western humanitarian framing in 1984-85 (Live Aid) treated the famine as an apolitical natural disaster; the political dimensions — Derg counterinsurgency withholding aid from Tigray, forced resettlement — were documented contemporaneously by MSF (expelled from Ethiopia in 1985 for saying so) but only fully acknowledged in international consensus after the 1991 HRW report.
ethical_notes: Classified OTHER rather than FAMINE alone because the political manipulation of the famine (counterinsurgency strategy) is constitutive of the event. Not classified GENOCIDE — scholarly consensus does not extend that far despite the ethnic dimension of the counterinsurgency.

entity_links.notes: imperial regime had fallen in 1974; Derg is successor not in entity list

sources.citation: de Waal, A. (1997). Famine Crimes: Politics and the Disaster Relief Industry in Africa. James Currey.
Human Rights Watch / Africa Watch (1991). Evil Days: 30 Years of War and Famine in Ethiopia. HRW.

location_name: Eastern Zaire / Democratic Republic of the Congo
main_actor: Alliance des Forces Démocratiques pour la Libération du Congo-Zaïre (AFDL) under Laurent-Désiré Kabila, backed by Rwanda (RPF), Uganda, Angola and Eritrea; against the regime of Mobutu Sese Seko
description: Triggered by the spillover of Rwandan Hutu génocidaires and refugees into eastern Zaire after July 1994, and the Mobutu regime's support for Hutu Interahamwe militias in North Kivu, Rwanda and Uganda sponsored the AFDL rebellion that crossed Zaire from east to west in seven months. Kabila entered Kinshasa on 17 May 1997 and renamed the country the Democratic Republic of the Congo. During the advance, Rwandan Patriotic Army units and AFDL troops conducted systematic massacres of Hutu refugees (documented in the 2010 UN Mapping Report as potentially constituting crimes of genocide). Total war deaths 1996-1997: 200,000 to 250,000.
casualties_source: UN OHCHR (2010). Democratic Republic of the Congo 1993-2003 Mapping Report
ethical_notes: The classification CONQUEST captures the regime-change character; the component massacres of Hutu refugees are assessed by the UN Mapping Report as potentially genocidal crimes (requiring judicial determination) but no court has ruled. Distinguished from the Second Congo War (1998-2003) catalogued separately.

entity_links.notes: Rwandan Patriotic Army as principal backer

sources.citation: Prunier, G. (2009). Africa's World War: Congo, the Rwandan Genocide, and the Making of a Continental Catastrophe. Oxford University Press.
UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (2010). Democratic Republic of the Congo 1993-2003: Report of the Mapping Exercise.
Reyntjens, F. (2009). The Great African War: Congo and Regional Geopolitics, 1996-2006. Cambridge University Press.

name_original: Intambara ya Kabiri ya Kongo / Africa's World War
location_name: Democratic Republic of the Congo (nationwide; Ituri, Kivu, Katanga, Équateur)
main_actor: Nine African states — DRC (Kabila), Angola, Zimbabwe, Namibia, Chad pro-government; Rwanda, Uganda, Burundi anti-government — plus 25+ militia groups (RCD factions, MLC, Mai-Mai, FDLR, Lendu-Hema militias)
description: After Laurent Kabila broke with his Rwandan and Ugandan backers in July 1998, Rwanda and Uganda sponsored the RCD rebellion against him; Angola, Zimbabwe and Namibia intervened on Kabila's side. The resulting war drew in nine African states and dozens of militias, fragmenting DRC into de facto zones of occupation exploited for coltan, gold and diamonds. Peace accords (Lusaka 1999, Pretoria 2002, Sun City 2003) ended formal hostilities; the International Rescue Committee mortality surveys (2000-2007) estimated 5.4 million excess deaths from violence, disease and starvation, making it the deadliest conflict since World War II. Ituri ethnic massacres (Lendu vs. Hema 1999-2003) led to the ICC's first conviction (Lubanga, 2012).
casualties_source: International Rescue Committee (2008). Mortality in the Democratic Republic of Congo: An Ongoing Crisis (critiqued by Human Security Report 2010)
silence_reason: The IRC 5.4 million figure is the most widely cited and was critiqued by the Human Security Report (2010) as overstated by baseline-mortality methodology; a revised estimate of 2.5 to 3 million is favoured by some demographers. The death toll is near-universally underrepresented in Western public memory compared to contemporaneous conflicts in Bosnia, Rwanda or Iraq. Rape as a weapon of war in Kivu was documented by Jocelyn Kelly (Harvard) and Denis Mukwege (2018 Nobel Peace Prize laureate) from 2005 onward.
ethical_notes: EventType enum lacks 'war' generically; classified OTHER with narrative description. The term 'Africa's World War' (Prunier 2009) reflects the nine-state scale. Not classified GENOCIDE at the aggregate level though specific sub-events (Kisangani 2000, Ituri massacres) may qualify.

sources.citation: Prunier, G. (2009). Africa's World War. Oxford University Press.
Stearns, J. (2011). Dancing in the Glory of Monsters: The Collapse of the Congo and the Great War of Africa. PublicAffairs.
International Rescue Committee (2008). Mortality in the Democratic Republic of Congo: An Ongoing Crisis.
Human Security Report Project (2010). The Shrinking Costs of War. Simon Fraser University.

location_name: Union Buildings, Pretoria, South Africa
main_actor: African National Congress (Nelson Mandela); National Party (F.W. de Klerk); Inkatha Freedom Party (Mangosuthu Buthelezi); negotiated transition via CODESA
description: On 26-29 April 1994 South Africa held its first non-racial general election, with 19.7 million votes cast (86% turnout); the ANC received 62.65%. On 10 May 1994 Nelson Mandela was inaugurated as the first president of a democratic South Africa, ending 46 years of statutory apartheid and 342 years of white minority rule. The Truth and Reconciliation Commission (chaired by Archbishop Desmond Tutu, 1996-1998) documented apartheid-era human-rights violations. The transition is one of very few negotiated ends to a settler-colonial regime.
ethical_notes: The 1994 transition is sometimes described as 'miraculous' or 'peaceful' — both claims elide the ~14,000 deaths in the 1990-1994 transition violence (notably the Third Force KwaZulu-Natal conflicts documented by the Goldstone Commission), and the economic inequality that was not addressed by the political transition. These caveats are essential to accurate representation.

entity_links.notes: transformation of the state

sources.citation: Sparks, A. (1995). Tomorrow Is Another Country: The Inside Story of South Africa's Road to Change. University of Chicago Press.
Mandela, N. (1994). Long Walk to Freedom. Macdonald Purnell.
Truth and Reconciliation Commission of South Africa (1998-2003). Report (7 vols.). Juta.

location_name: Darfur, western Sudan
main_actor: Government of Sudan under President Omar al-Bashir; Janjaweed Arab militias; against SLA/M and JEM rebel groups and the Fur, Masalit and Zaghawa civilian populations
description: In February 2003 the Sudan Liberation Army (SLA) and Justice and Equality Movement (JEM) rose in Darfur against the Khartoum government. Khartoum's response — aerial bombardment coordinated with Janjaweed ground militias drawn from Arabised Sudanese pastoralists — systematically destroyed Fur, Masalit and Zaghawa villages. The US State Department formally determined genocide on 9 September 2004 (Powell testimony to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee). The International Criminal Court indicted President al-Bashir for genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes in 2009-2010 (first sitting head of state so indicted). Mortality estimates 300,000 to 400,000; displacement 2.5 to 2.7 million.
casualties_source: Degomme, O. & Guha-Sapir, D. (2010). 'Patterns of mortality rates in Darfur conflict', Lancet 375: 294-300
ethical_notes: GENOCIDE classification per ETHICS-007 reflects the US State Department determination (2004), ICC indictment (2009-2010), and predominantly Fur/Masalit/Zaghawa target selection. Some observers (e.g. UN Commission of Inquiry 2005) argued for 'crimes against humanity' rather than genocide; the ICC judges accepted the genocide charge on reconsideration in 2010. Casualty range: lower estimates emphasise direct violence, higher estimates include displacement-induced mortality.

entity_links.notes: successor region to the Fur sultanate
Sudanese state (modern Republic of Sudan not in entity list; Mahdiyya used as closest historical Sudanese polity in DB)

sources.citation: Prunier, G. (2005). Darfur: The Ambiguous Genocide. Hurst.
de Waal, A. & Flint, J. (2008). Darfur: A New History of a Long War. Zed Books.
International Criminal Court, Prosecutor v. Omar Hassan Ahmad Al Bashir, ICC-02/05-01/09, Second Decision on the Prosecution's Application for a Warrant of Arrest, 12 July 2010.
Degomme, O. & Guha-Sapir, D. (2010). 'Patterns of mortality rates in Darfur conflict'. Lancet 375(9711): 294-300.

name_original: Révolution haïtienne et abolition dans les empires européens
location_name: Atlantic abolition cascade — Britain (1807/1833), France (1848), Brazil (1888)
main_actor: British Parliament (Slave Trade Act 1807, Slavery Abolition Act 1833); French Second Republic (Schoelcher decree 1848); Brazilian Parliament (Lei Áurea 1888)
description: The Haitian Revolution (1791-1804, catalogued separately) discredited the economic and moral viability of plantation slavery in the French and Spanish Atlantic. Britain abolished the trans-Atlantic slave trade in 1807 and emancipated slaves in its Caribbean colonies in 1833 (with £20 million compensation to slave-owners, not to the enslaved). The French Second Republic abolished slavery in 1848 under Victor Schoelcher. Brazil's Lei Áurea of 13 May 1888 was the last major abolition in the Atlantic world. Abolition of the slave trade in international law did not end slavery in West Africa itself, which persisted under French and British colonial 'protectorate' regimes into the twentieth century.
ethical_notes: The 'abolition' narrative in European historiography foregrounds British Evangelical agency (Wilberforce et al.) and erases the causal role of the Haitian Revolution and of enslaved resistance across the plantation Atlantic. Drescher (1977) and Blackburn (1988) established the revisionist economic history. The compensation of slave-owners rather than the enslaved (British 1833 £20 million ≈ 40% of Treasury expenditure) is a standing moral fact of the abolition regime.

entity_links.notes: United Kingdom from 1707/1801

sources.citation: Blackburn, R. (1988). The Overthrow of Colonial Slavery, 1776-1848. Verso.
Drescher, S. (1977). Econocide: British Slavery in the Era of Abolition. University of Pittsburgh Press.
Costa, E.V. da (2000). The Brazilian Empire: Myths and Histories. University of North Carolina Press.

name_original: معركة أم درمان / Battle of Omdurman
location_name: Omdurman, Sudan
main_actor: Anglo-Egyptian expeditionary force under Major-General Herbert Kitchener; Mahdist Sudan under Khalifa Abdullahi
description: On 2 September 1898 a 26,000-strong Anglo-Egyptian force equipped with Maxim guns, artillery, and gunboats defeated approximately 52,000 Mahdist Ansar forces outside Omdurman. Mahdist dead were 9,700 to 12,000 with 13,000 wounded and 5,000 captured; Anglo-Egyptian losses were 48 killed and 382 wounded. After the battle Kitchener ordered the exhumation and desecration of the Mahdi's tomb; the Mahdi's skull was taken as a trophy (returned and reburied after Queen Victoria's intervention). The victory ended the Mahdist state and incorporated Sudan into the British Empire as Anglo-Egyptian Condominium.
ethical_notes: Catalogued COLONIAL_VIOLENCE rather than BATTLE because the overwhelming asymmetry of weaponry (Maxim guns vs. spears and muzzle-loaders) and the killing of wounded on the field (witnessed and protested by Churchill) define the engagement's character. The desecration of the Mahdi's tomb is a documented colonial war crime.

sources.citation: Churchill, W.S. (1899). The River War: An Account of the Reconquest of the Soudan. Longmans.
Keown-Boyd, H. (1986). A Good Dusting: The Sudan Campaigns, 1883-1899. Leo Cooper.

location_name: Hausaland, modern northern Nigeria
main_actor: Shehu Usuman dan Fodio, Fulani Islamic scholar-reformer; against the Hausa kingdoms of Gobir, Kano, Katsina, Zazzau, Zamfara and others
description: In February 1804 Usuman dan Fodio of the Qadiriyya Sufi order declared jihad against the Hausa kings of Gobir, accusing them of mixing Islam with pre-Islamic practice and of oppressive taxation. Within four years his Fulani-led coalition overthrew the major Hausa city-states and established the Sokoto Caliphate, which endured as the largest precolonial African state in the nineteenth century until British conquest in 1903. The caliphate organised some 30 emirates, codified Maliki Islamic law, and became the leading centre of Islamic scholarship in West Africa.
ethical_notes: The Sokoto Caliphate as successor polity operated slave plantations (rinji) on a massive scale — 1 to 2.5 million enslaved persons by the late nineteenth century, the second-largest slave society of the period after the United States South. This is a documented part of the record and is not elided.

entity_links.notes: Sokoto Caliphate
Seven Hausa states supplanted

sources.citation: Last, M. (1967). The Sokoto Caliphate. Longman.
Lovejoy, P.E. (2016). Jihad in West Africa during the Age of Revolutions. Ohio University Press.

name_original: Scramble for Africa / Partage de l'Afrique
location_name: African continent (all regions except Ethiopia and Liberia)
main_actor: European powers — Britain, France, Germany, Belgium, Portugal, Italy, Spain — following the 1884-1885 Berlin Conference framework
description: Between the French occupation of Tunisia (1881) and the outbreak of World War I (1914), European powers partitioned the African continent. In 1870 Africa was ~10% under European control; by 1914 this had reached ~90% (Ethiopia and Liberia excepted). Major campaigns included French conquest of the western Sahel (Mali-Niger-Chad axis, 1880s-1900s with Voulet-Chanoine mission atrocities 1898-1899), British conquest of Egypt (1882), Sudan (1896-1898), Asante (1900), Benin (1897), the Anglo-Boer Wars (1880-1881, 1899-1902), German conquest of South West Africa and East Africa (1884-1907), Italian failures in Ethiopia (1896) and conquest of Libya (1911-1912), Portuguese consolidation in Angola and Mozambique (Gungunhana war 1895), and Belgian absorption of the Congo Free State (1908).
casualties_source: Pakenham, T. (1991). The Scramble for Africa (aggregate; difficult to distinguish from demographic decline due to famine, disease, labour regimes)
ethical_notes: Aggregate CONQUEST entry; specific component events (Berlin Conference 1884 already catalogued, Congo Free State 1885-1908 catalogued, Herero/Nama genocide 1904 catalogued, Second Italo-Ethiopian War 1935) are separate records. Casualty range reflects the difficulty of separating conquest violence from downstream famines (e.g. 1896-1902 Ethiopian famine, 1898-1899 East African famine). Some scholars (Sven Lindqvist, Mike Davis) frame the scramble as 'late Victorian holocaust' encompassing ~30 to 50 million deaths continent-wide when indirect causes are fully counted.

sources.citation: Pakenham, T. (1991). The Scramble for Africa, 1876-1912. Random House.
Wesseling, H.L. (1996). Divide and Rule: The Partition of Africa, 1880-1914. Praeger.
Davis, M. (2001). Late Victorian Holocausts: El Niño Famines and the Making of the Third World. Verso.

name_original: فتح مراكش / Conquête française du Maroc
location_name: Morocco (from coastal Casablanca to the Anti-Atlas and Rif)
main_actor: Republique francaise (protectorate from 1912 under General Lyautey); Kingdom of Spain (northern protectorate); Moroccan resistance under Rif leader Abd el-Krim and Atlas chieftains
description: The French invasion began at Casablanca (August 1907) following the murder of European workers; the Treaty of Fez (30 March 1912) established the French Protectorate under Sultan Abd al-Hafid's signature. The Rif War (1921-1926) saw the tribal confederation under Abd el-Krim defeat a Spanish army at Annual (July 1921, ~13,000 Spanish dead) and declare the Republic of the Rif; combined Franco-Spanish force under Marshal Pétain and General Primo de Rivera suppressed the Rif Republic in 1925-1926 using mustard gas (tested on civilians). Atlas resistance under various qaids continued until the fall of the Anti-Atlas pockets in 1934.
silence_reason: Spanish use of chemical weapons in the Rif (yperite bombs dropped on civilian markets) was officially denied until documentary evidence from German and French archives was published (Balfour 2002; Madariaga 2005). The French protectorate period is often described as Lyautey's 'protectorate-building' which elides the military-conquest character of the 1907-1934 phase.
ethical_notes: Classified CONQUEST given the 27-year arms duration. The chemical-weapon dimension (Spanish Rif campaign 1924-26) may justify a follow-on COLONIAL_VIOLENCE record.

entity_links.notes: reduced to nominal sovereignty under protectorate

sources.citation: Pennell, C.R. (2000). Morocco since 1830: A History. New York University Press.
Balfour, S. (2002). Deadly Embrace: Morocco and the Road to the Spanish Civil War. Oxford University Press.
Madariaga, M.R. de (2005). En el barranco del lobo: las guerras de Marruecos. Alianza.

name_original: Afrikalı mülayih ve ilgâ-yı ubûdiyet-i âlât / End of Zanzibar slave trade
location_name: Zanzibar, East African coast
main_actor: Sultan Barghash bin Said of Zanzibar under British pressure (Frere Treaty); British Royal Navy East Africa Squadron
description: After centuries of the Indian Ocean slave trade centred on Zanzibar — where 10,000 to 20,000 enslaved Africans were sold annually in the 1860s, mostly to clove plantations on Zanzibar itself and on Pemba — Sultan Barghash signed the Frere Treaty on 5 June 1873 under threat of Royal Navy blockade, closing the public slave market at Stone Town. Enforcement was partial: caravan routes from Lake Tanganyika to the coast remained active into the 1890s, documented by the Brussels Anti-Slavery Conference (1889-1890). The East African clove and ivory economy continued to depend on slave labour until German and British colonial administrations gradually substituted corvée and wage regimes.
silence_reason: The Indian Ocean slave trade was historically underreported compared to the Atlantic trade despite probably exceeding 3 to 4 million persons between 1600 and 1900 (Sheriff 1987; Clarence-Smith 2006). The caravan routes across present-day Tanzania and Congo collapsed the demographies of the Manyema and Nyamwezi in ways that remained invisible until Livingstone's 1871 dispatches and later colonial-era demographic surveys.
ethical_notes: Classified SLAVE_TRADE per ETHICS-007. The 1873 Frere Treaty is the formal endpoint of the legal market; the trade itself persisted illegally. Casualty fields null because no single-event death count is meaningful; aggregate mortality on East African caravans (Beachey 1976) is estimated at 3 to 4 deaths per captive reaching the coast.

entity_links.notes: imposing pressure via Royal Navy

sources.citation: Sheriff, A. (1987). Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar: Integration of an East African Commercial Empire into the World Economy, 1770-1873. James Currey.
Clarence-Smith, W.G. (2006). Islam and the Abolition of Slavery. Oxford University Press.
Beachey, R.W. (1976). The Slave Trade of Eastern Africa. Collings.
